title: AI/ML Bias & Fairness
subtitle: Who Teaches the Machine — And What Does It Learn?
overview: Students explore how AI and Machine Learning systems can unintentionally learn bias from data — and why fairness in AI matters for everyone. Through real-world examples and discussions, students learn to think critically about who builds AI, what data it learns from, and how biased AI can impact real people's lives.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — whiteboard discussions, bias activity results, student debate moments, AI ethics diagrams.

keyTopics: What is AI Bias? — When AI makes unfair decisions because of skewed training data
Training Data — AI learns from examples; biased examples = biased AI
Garbage In, Garbage Out — Bad data produces bad AI results
Real-World Bias Examples — Face recognition, hiring algorithms, loan approvals
Types of Bias — Data bias, historical bias, representation bias
Who is Affected? — How AI bias impacts real people unfairly
Fairness in AI — What does it mean for an AI to be fair?
How to Reduce Bias — Diverse data, diverse teams, testing and auditing AI
Human Responsibility — Humans build AI; humans must make it fair
Critical Thinking — Questioning AI decisions and not blindly trusting them

activity.title: Spot the Bias — AI Fairness Challenge
parts: Activity sheet with AI scenario cards
steps: Read the scenario: An AI is trained to shortlist job applicants using old hiring data
Discuss: What patterns might the old data contain? Who might get unfairly rejected?
Read scenario 2: A face recognition AI was trained mostly on light-skinned faces
Discuss: Who will this AI work well for? Who might it fail?
Activity: In groups, design a 'fair' AI training dataset for a school admission system
List: What types of students must be represented in your data?
Present your dataset plan to the class — explain your fairness choices
Optional: Use Teachable Machine — train with unequal data, observe biased results
Class reflection: Who is responsible when AI makes an unfair decision?

options: When an AI runs too slowly
When an AI makes unfair or skewed decisions due to biased training data
When an AI cannot connect to the internet
When a programmer makes a typing mistake
quiz.explanation: AI bias happens when the data used to train an AI contains unfair patterns — and the AI learns and repeats those unfair patterns in its decisions.

options: Its own imagination
Only from books
Training data — examples provided by humans
The internet only
quiz.explanation: AI learns from training data — examples humans feed it. If those examples are biased or unrepresentative, the AI will learn and repeat that bias.

quiz.q: What does 'Garbage In, Garbage Out' mean in AI?
options: AI systems produce physical waste
Bad or biased input data leads to bad or biased AI outputs
AI should be stored in a bin when not in use
Old computers give wrong answers
quiz.explanation: Garbage In, Garbage Out means the quality of AI output depends entirely on the quality of input data. Feed it biased data → get biased results!

quiz.q: A hiring AI was trained on 20 years of old company data where most employees were men. What bias might it learn?
options: It will prefer younger candidates
It will prefer candidates from big cities
It may unfairly rank male applicants higher than female applicants
It will only hire people with glasses
quiz.explanation: Historical bias! If past data shows mostly men were hired, the AI learns that pattern and may unfairly favour male applicants — even if women are equally or more qualified.

quiz.q: What is 'representation bias' in AI training data?
options: When the AI is trained too fast
When certain groups of people are missing or underrepresented in the training data
When the AI represents data using charts
When the programmer is biased against coding
quiz.explanation: Representation bias occurs when some groups are barely included in training data. If a face recognition AI is mostly trained on light-skinned faces, it will perform poorly on darker-skinned faces.

quiz.q: A face recognition AI works well for light-skinned people but often misidentifies dark-skinned people. This is an example of:
options: A software crash
Normal AI behaviour
AI bias caused by unrepresentative training data
A hardware problem
quiz.explanation: This is AI bias! The training data likely had far fewer dark-skinned faces, so the AI never learned to recognise them accurately. This has real harmful consequences in policing and security.

quiz.q: Which of these is a real-world area where AI bias has caused harm?
options: Weather forecasting
Loan approvals and hiring decisions
Calculating the speed of light
Sorting a list of numbers
quiz.explanation: AI bias has been found in loan approval systems, hiring algorithms, criminal risk scoring and facial recognition — affecting real people's access to jobs, money and justice.

quiz.q: What is one of the best ways to reduce bias in an AI system?
options: Make the AI run faster
Use more diverse and representative training data
Train the AI on less data
Remove all human involvement
quiz.explanation: Diverse, representative data is key! If your training data includes people of all genders, ages, ethnicities and backgrounds, the AI learns fairer patterns.

quiz.q: Why does having diverse teams of AI developers help reduce bias?
options: More people means faster coding
Diverse teams are more likely to notice when data or decisions are unfair to certain groups
It is a legal requirement in all countries
It has no effect on AI bias
quiz.explanation: Diverse teams bring different perspectives! If everyone building the AI has the same background, they may not notice when the system is unfair to groups different from themselves.

quiz.q: An AI loan approval system rejects more applications from people in certain neighbourhoods. This could be because:
options: The AI prefers round numbers
The training data contained historical patterns of discrimination against those areas
The server is located far away
The applicants used the wrong browser
quiz.explanation: Historical bias! If past loan data reflects old discriminatory lending practices, the AI learns those patterns and repeats the same discrimination — now automated at massive scale.

quiz.q: What does it mean for an AI to be 'fair'?
options: It runs without crashing
It treats all people equitably regardless of gender, race, age or background
It gives the same answer to every question
It is available for free
quiz.explanation: A fair AI treats all groups equitably — its accuracy and decisions should not vary significantly based on a person's gender, race, age, or socioeconomic background.

quiz.q: Who is ultimately responsible when an AI makes an unfair or harmful decision?
options: The AI itself — it made the decision
Nobody — it was an accident
The humans who designed, built and deployed the AI
The user who clicked the button
quiz.explanation: Humans are responsible! AI doesn't think or feel — it does what it was trained to do. The designers, data collectors and companies deploying AI must be accountable for its impact.

quiz.q: What should you do if an AI makes a decision that seems unfair to you or someone else?
options: Accept it — AI is always correct
Question it, ask for a human review, and report it
Ignore it and move on
Blame the internet connection
quiz.explanation: Always question AI decisions, especially high-stakes ones! You have the right to ask for a human review. Critical thinking about AI outputs is an essential skill for everyone.

options: When AI is used to study history
When AI training data reflects past human prejudices and discrimination
When old computers are used to train AI
When AI makes mistakes about historical dates
quiz.explanation: Historical bias means the training data carries the prejudices of the past — like old hiring records, criminal justice data, or lending patterns — and the AI learns to repeat those injustices.

quiz.q: Why is AI bias considered an important social issue, not just a technical one?
options: Because it slows down computers
Because biased AI affects real people's lives — jobs, loans, justice and opportunities
Because it uses too much electricity
Because programmers find it boring to fix
quiz.explanation: AI bias is a social justice issue! When biased AI is used in hiring, lending, healthcare or policing, it can deny real people real opportunities — often affecting already marginalised communities the most.
